problems.name: WiFi Disconnection
problems.description: IoT device repeatedly drops its WiFi connection, causing interruptions in data reporting and remote control.
visual_symptoms: Device LED indicator flashing error pattern instead of solid connected state
Device offline status shown in cloud dashboard
Periodic gaps in sensor data time series
Device unresponsive to remote commands intermittently

probable_causes.cause: Weak WiFi signal at device location causing frequent deauthentication
probable_causes.test: Measure RSSI at device location — below -70 dBm is insufficient for stable connection

probable_causes.cause: Router DHCP lease expiry or IP conflict causing disconnection
probable_causes.test: Check router DHCP logs for lease conflicts; assign static IP to device MAC address

probable_causes.cause: WiFi driver bug in firmware causing periodic stack overflow and reset
probable_causes.test: Check device serial log for WiFi driver exception or watchdog reset at disconnection time

steps.instruction: Log into router admin panel and assign a static DHCP reservation to the device MAC address
steps.verification: Device always receives same IP; no DHCP conflict entries in router log

steps.instruction: Measure WiFi signal strength at device location using a phone WiFi analyser; reposition device or add a WiFi extender if RSSI is below -70 dBm
steps.verification: RSSI at device location is -65 dBm or better

steps.instruction: Update device firmware to latest version to benefit from WiFi driver stability fixes
steps.verification: Device uptime in dashboard exceeds 24 hours without reconnection event

problems.name: MQTT Broker Unreachable
problems.description: IoT device cannot connect to the MQTT broker, preventing all message publishing and subscription.
visual_symptoms: Device log shows repeated MQTT connection refused or timeout errors
No messages arriving on broker topic subscriptions
Device cloud status shows disconnected with broker error code
Alert: all devices offline simultaneously

probable_causes.cause: MQTT broker service stopped or crashed on server
probable_causes.test: SSH into broker host and run 'systemctl status mosquitto' or equivalent; check process is running

probable_causes.cause: Firewall rule blocking port 1883 or 8883 between device and broker
probable_causes.test: Run 'telnet <broker_ip> 1883' from device network — connection refused indicates firewall block

probable_causes.cause: Incorrect broker hostname or port in device configuration after network change
probable_causes.test: Compare device configured broker address with actual broker IP; check if broker IP changed after router reset

steps.instruction: SSH into broker server and restart the MQTT service: 'systemctl restart mosquitto'
steps.verification: Service status shows 'active (running)'; broker accepts test connection from MQTT client tool

steps.instruction: Verify firewall rules allow inbound TCP on port 1883 from device subnet; add rule if missing
steps.verification: Telnet or netcat to broker port 1883 succeeds from device network

steps.instruction: Update broker hostname or IP in device configuration if it has changed; reflash or OTA update the config
steps.verification: Device connects to broker and publishes test message within 30 seconds of boot

problems.name: Sensor Calibration Drift
problems.description: Sensor readings gradually deviate from true values over time due to environmental factors or component ageing.
visual_symptoms: Sensor readings consistently offset from reference instrument by fixed amount
Readings drift upward or downward monotonically over days or weeks
Sensor output exceeds rated operating range despite normal environment
Two identical sensors in same location show diverging readings

probable_causes.cause: Sensor exposed to conditions outside rated operating range causing permanent offset
probable_causes.test: Compare sensor datasheet operating range with actual measured temperature/humidity at sensor location

probable_causes.cause: Reference voltage source on ADC input drifting due to component ageing
probable_causes.test: Measure ADC reference voltage with calibrated multimeter; compare to design specification

probable_causes.cause: Contamination of sensor element from dust, chemicals or humidity
probable_causes.test: Visually inspect sensor membrane or element; compare readings before and after gentle cleaning

steps.instruction: Place a calibrated reference sensor alongside the device sensor and record both readings for 24 hours
steps.verification: Systematic offset or drift pattern quantified from reference comparison data

steps.instruction: Apply a software calibration offset or scale factor in firmware to correct the measured drift
steps.verification: Corrected readings match reference sensor within stated accuracy specification

steps.instruction: If drift exceeds 10% of full scale, replace sensor element and repeat calibration procedure
steps.verification: New sensor readings within specification without software correction

problems.name: OTA Firmware Update Failure
problems.description: Over-the-air firmware update fails mid-process, leaving device in an inconsistent or non-booting state.
visual_symptoms: Device stuck in boot loop after update attempt
Update progress stops at specific percentage and device reboots
OTA update error code in cloud dashboard for device
Device no longer appears online after update was initiated

probable_causes.cause: WiFi disconnection during firmware download causing partial write to flash
probable_causes.test: Check device log for network errors during OTA download phase; verify WiFi signal strength

probable_causes.cause: Insufficient flash partition size for new firmware image
probable_causes.test: Compare new firmware binary size with OTA partition size defined in partition table

probable_causes.cause: Firmware image checksum mismatch due to corrupted download
probable_causes.test: Re-download firmware image from server and verify SHA256 checksum matches release manifest

steps.instruction: If device boots to recovery partition, trigger OTA retry from cloud dashboard or serial console
steps.verification: Device enters OTA update mode and begins download

steps.instruction: If device is completely unresponsive, connect via UART serial and flash firmware using esptool or platform-specific flasher
steps.verification: Flash write completes at 100%; device boots to normal firmware

steps.instruction: Verify OTA partition size in partition table accommodates new firmware; update partition table if necessary before next OTA
steps.verification: New firmware size is at least 10% smaller than OTA partition; update succeeds without truncation

problems.name: Factory Reset Loop
problems.description: Device repeatedly performs factory reset on boot due to corrupted configuration storage, unable to retain settings.
visual_symptoms: Device requires re-pairing every time it is power cycled
All configured settings lost after each reboot
Factory reset LED pattern on every startup
Device provisioning log shows NVS (non-volatile storage) erase on each boot

probable_causes.cause: NVS flash partition corrupted causing firmware to trigger automatic reset
probable_causes.test: Read NVS partition via serial console; check for filesystem errors or blank pages where config should exist

probable_causes.cause: Hardware reset button or GPIO line stuck in asserted state triggering reset logic
probable_causes.test: Measure voltage on reset GPIO pin at boot; should not assert for more than 200 ms

probable_causes.cause: Firmware bug treating invalid config version as signal to factory reset
probable_causes.test: Check firmware changelog for known factory-reset-loop bugs in current version

steps.instruction: Connect via serial console and erase the NVS partition manually using esptool: 'esptool.py erase_region <nvs_offset> <nvs_size>'
steps.verification: NVS partition fully erased; device boots without triggering factory reset

steps.instruction: Update to latest firmware version which may include NVS corruption recovery logic
steps.verification: Device retains configuration across 5 consecutive power cycles

steps.instruction: Inspect hardware for stuck reset button or moisture on reset GPIO; clean or replace button if mechanically stuck
steps.verification: Reset GPIO reads high (logic 1) at boot; no unintended reset assertion detected

problems.name: Power Supply Noise
problems.description: Electrical noise on the power supply rail causes erratic sensor readings, random resets, or communication errors.
visual_symptoms: Sensor readings spike randomly without environmental change
Device resets at high load events such as WiFi transmission
Oscilloscope shows ripple over 100 mV peak-to-peak on 3.3V rail
ADC measurements show high variance on stable signal source

probable_causes.cause: Insufficient bulk capacitance on power rail causing voltage sag during WiFi TX burst
probable_causes.test: Scope 3.3V rail during WiFi transmission; sag below 3.0V during burst confirms insufficient bulk capacitance

probable_causes.cause: Switching power supply EMI coupling onto signal lines sharing ground plane
probable_causes.test: Replace switching supply with linear LDO on test bench; observe if noise and resets disappear

probable_causes.cause: Ground loop between device and peripherals causing common-mode noise injection
probable_causes.test: Disconnect all peripheral cables one at a time; identify if noise correlates with specific peripheral

steps.instruction: Add a 470 uF electrolytic capacitor in parallel with a 100 nF ceramic capacitor on the 3.3V rail near the MCU
steps.verification: Oscilloscope shows voltage sag during WiFi burst reduced to less than 50 mV

steps.instruction: Add a ferrite bead on the power supply input line to filter high-frequency switching noise
steps.verification: High-frequency ripple above 1 MHz reduced by at least 20 dB measured on scope

steps.instruction: Ensure separate ground planes for power and signal sections; use star grounding topology at single point
steps.verification: ADC noise floor reduced; variance on stable reference input below 2 LSB

problems.name: Memory Leak in Firmware
problems.description: Firmware gradually consumes all available heap memory, eventually causing a watchdog reset or undefined behaviour.
visual_symptoms: Device uptime resets every few hours or days regularly
Heap free memory decreasing monotonically in telemetry
Device becomes unresponsive before watchdog reset
Log shows heap allocation failure errors before crash

probable_causes.cause: Dynamic memory allocated in task loop without corresponding free call
probable_causes.test: Instrument code with heap_caps_get_free_size() logging every minute; identify which task period correlates with memory decrease

probable_causes.cause: String or buffer dynamically allocated on MQTT/HTTP received data not freed after processing
probable_causes.test: Review all malloc/new calls in network receive callbacks; trace each allocation to its free counterpart

probable_causes.cause: Third-party library leaking memory on specific error paths
probable_causes.test: Comment out third-party library usage and observe if leak persists; update library to latest version

steps.instruction: Enable heap trace using ESP-IDF heap tracing or equivalent RTOS tool; run device for 30 minutes and dump trace
steps.verification: Heap trace output identifies unfreed allocation sites with call stack

steps.instruction: Fix identified allocation sites by adding matching free() or delete calls on all code paths including error exits
steps.verification: Heap free size remains stable (less than 1% change) over 24 hours of operation

steps.instruction: As interim mitigation, implement a scheduled safe restart every 48 hours using RTC alarm or NTP-triggered reboot
steps.verification: Device restarts cleanly at scheduled time and reconnects within 30 seconds

problems.name: SSL Certificate Expiry
problems.description: TLS certificate on MQTT broker or HTTPS endpoint expires, causing all devices to reject the connection.
visual_symptoms: All devices show TLS handshake failure simultaneously
Device log shows SSL_ERROR_CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED
Cloud dashboard shows all devices offline at same timestamp
Certificate expiry date matches current date in server certificate

probable_causes.cause: Server TLS certificate expired due to missed renewal
probable_causes.test: Run 'openssl s_client -connect <broker>:8883' and check certificate Not After date

probable_causes.cause: Device CA root certificate bundle outdated and does not include new certificate authority
probable_causes.test: Compare device CA bundle with current Mozilla root store; check if signing CA is present in device bundle

probable_causes.cause: System clock on device incorrect causing valid certificate to appear expired
probable_causes.test: Print device RTC time to serial log; compare with actual UTC time — discrepancy of months indicates clock error

steps.instruction: Renew TLS certificate on broker server using Let's Encrypt certbot or CA provider; reload broker service
steps.verification: 'openssl s_client' shows new certificate with Not After date at least 90 days in future

steps.instruction: Set up automatic certificate renewal via cron: '0 12 * * * certbot renew --quiet'
steps.verification: Certbot dry-run completes successfully; cron job visible in crontab listing

steps.instruction: Push updated CA bundle to devices via OTA update if root CA has changed
steps.verification: All devices reconnect successfully; TLS handshake completes without error in device log

problems.name: Hub Pairing Failure
problems.description: IoT device fails to pair with the hub or gateway during provisioning, remaining inaccessible from the app.
visual_symptoms: Device LED shows pairing mode but hub app reports no device found
Pairing timeout error in hub app after full wait period
Device visible in WiFi scan but app cannot complete provisioning
Hub log shows rejected association from device MAC address

probable_causes.cause: Device and hub on different network subnets preventing mDNS or broadcast discovery
probable_causes.test: Confirm phone, hub and device are all on same 2.4 GHz WiFi SSID during pairing

probable_causes.cause: Hub firmware and device firmware using incompatible pairing protocol versions
probable_causes.test: Check hub firmware version in app and compare with device minimum hub version requirement in product documentation

probable_causes.cause: Hub at maximum paired device capacity refusing new associations
probable_causes.test: Check number of paired devices in hub app; compare with documented maximum device limit

steps.instruction: Ensure phone, hub and device are all connected to the same 2.4 GHz WiFi network; disable 5 GHz temporarily if router combines both bands
steps.verification: All three devices shown on same subnet in router DHCP table

steps.instruction: Update hub firmware to latest version via hub app before retrying pairing
steps.verification: Hub firmware version shows latest release number; pairing mode initiated fresh

steps.instruction: Factory reset the device and retry pairing within 60 seconds of reset
steps.verification: Device paired successfully; appears in hub app device list with online status

problems.name: Zigbee Mesh Fragmentation
problems.description: Zigbee network splits into disconnected clusters, with some devices no longer reachable from the coordinator.
visual_symptoms: Subset of Zigbee devices show offline despite being powered
Network map in coordinator app shows isolated device islands
Commands to unreachable devices time out consistently
Devices rejoin network temporarily then drop off again

probable_causes.cause: Router device removed or powered off, severing mesh path for child devices
probable_causes.test: Review network topology map — identify which router device is missing and which end devices are orphaned

probable_causes.cause: Interference on Zigbee channel (2.4 GHz overlap with WiFi channels 1, 6, or 11)
probable_causes.test: Use Zigbee channel analyser to check PER (packet error rate) on current channel; compare with alternate channels

probable_causes.cause: Coordinator firmware bug causing incorrect routing table entries
probable_causes.test: Restart coordinator and observe if orphaned devices rejoin automatically within 5 minutes

steps.instruction: Restore or replace the missing router device to re-establish mesh path; add additional router devices if coverage is insufficient
steps.verification: Network topology map shows all previously offline devices reconnected

steps.instruction: Change Zigbee coordinator channel to 15, 20, 25, or 26 to avoid WiFi 2.4 GHz overlap
steps.verification: PER on new channel below 5%; all devices rejoin on new channel within 10 minutes

steps.instruction: Update coordinator firmware and rebuild network by re-pairing fragmented devices
steps.verification: All devices show online and respond to commands within 2 seconds

problems.name: DHCP IP Conflict
problems.description: Two devices on the network receive the same IP address, causing intermittent connectivity failures for both.
visual_symptoms: Device goes offline periodically without rebooting
Router ARP table shows two different MAC addresses for same IP
Cloud dashboard shows device toggling between online and offline
Network scan tools report duplicate IP warning

probable_causes.cause: DHCP server assigned same address to two devices due to exhausted lease pool
probable_causes.test: Check DHCP pool size in router settings versus total number of devices; count remaining available leases

probable_causes.cause: Static IP configured on device conflicts with DHCP range
probable_causes.test: Check if device has a hardcoded static IP within the router DHCP range; compare device IP with DHCP pool boundaries

probable_causes.cause: Stale DHCP lease not released by previous device leaving network
probable_causes.test: Review router DHCP lease table for expired leases not yet cleared; check lease duration setting

steps.instruction: Log into router admin panel and expand DHCP pool range or reduce lease time to reclaim unused addresses
steps.verification: DHCP pool has at least 20 free addresses; no duplicate IP entries in lease table

steps.instruction: Assign static DHCP reservations for all IoT devices using their MAC addresses
steps.verification: Each device MAC has a unique reserved IP; no conflicts in DHCP reservation list

steps.instruction: Power cycle all conflicting devices to force DHCP renegotiation with new addresses
steps.verification: All devices online with unique IPs confirmed in router ARP table

problems.name: NTP Sync Failure
problems.description: Device cannot synchronise time with NTP server, causing incorrect timestamps on sensor data and TLS certificate validation failures.
visual_symptoms: Device log timestamps diverging from real time by minutes or hours
TLS connections failing due to certificate time validation error
Sensor data timestamps in cloud dashboard showing wrong times
Device log shows NTP request timeout or unreachable server

probable_causes.cause: UDP port 123 blocked by firewall between device and NTP server
probable_causes.test: Test UDP connectivity to time.cloudflare.com:123 using netcat or UDP probe tool from device network

probable_causes.cause: NTP server hostname not resolving due to DNS configuration error
probable_causes.test: Verify DNS resolution of NTP hostname from device network; switch to IP address as fallback

probable_causes.cause: RTC hardware failure causing time to reset to epoch on every boot
probable_causes.test: Set time manually and power cycle; if time resets to 1970-01-01, RTC battery or module has failed

steps.instruction: Update device firmware to use multiple fallback NTP servers: pool.ntp.org, time.cloudflare.com, time.google.com
steps.verification: Device successfully syncs time from at least one server within 30 seconds of network connection

steps.instruction: Configure router/firewall to allow UDP port 123 outbound from IoT VLAN to internet
steps.verification: NTP sync succeeds; device log shows 'NTP time sync OK' with correct timestamp

steps.instruction: Replace RTC module battery if RTC resets to epoch on every power cycle
steps.verification: Device retains correct time across power cycles without NTP resync

problems.name: Watchdog Timer Reboot
problems.description: Device reboots unexpectedly due to watchdog timer expiry, caused by a blocked task preventing the watchdog from being fed.
visual_symptoms: Device reboots every few minutes under specific conditions
Boot log shows 'WDT reset' as reset reason
Device becomes unresponsive during specific operations before rebooting
Crash dump shows task stack overflow or blocked task

probable_causes.cause: Blocking I/O call or infinite loop in main task preventing watchdog feed
probable_causes.test: Add watchdog-triggered core dump to identify blocked task name and stack trace at time of WDT expiry

probable_causes.cause: Watchdog timeout set too short for legitimate long-running operations
probable_causes.test: Compare watchdog timeout value with maximum legitimate blocking duration of all tasks

probable_causes.cause: Stack overflow in high-priority task corrupting watchdog feed mechanism
probable_causes.test: Enable stack overflow detection in RTOS; check for stack overflow notifications in log before WDT reset

steps.instruction: Enable core dump to flash on WDT reset; analyse dump with xtensa-gdb or equivalent to identify blocked task
steps.verification: Core dump analysis identifies specific function and line number causing block

steps.instruction: Replace blocking synchronous calls with async equivalents or move them to a separate RTOS task with adequate stack size
steps.verification: Watchdog reboot no longer occurs under previously failing conditions

steps.instruction: Increase task stack sizes for all tasks that perform network or flash operations to prevent overflow
steps.verification: Minimum free stack watermark above 512 bytes for all tasks after 24 hours of operation

problems.name: High Power Consumption
problems.description: Device consumes significantly more power than expected, reducing battery life or exceeding power supply capacity.
visual_symptoms: Battery-powered device draining much faster than rated life
Power supply runs hot indicating near-overload condition
Current measurement significantly exceeds device datasheet specification
Deep-sleep mode not reducing current as expected

probable_causes.cause: Deep-sleep mode not entered due to GPIO hold configuration error
probable_causes.test: Measure current in intended sleep mode with multimeter; above 1 mA on ESP32 deep sleep indicates sleep not entered

probable_causes.cause: Peripheral left powered on during sleep — sensor, display or external module not powered down
probable_causes.test: Power peripherals via GPIO-controlled transistor switch; measure current with and without peripheral power switch

probable_causes.cause: Firmware polling loop running at too-high frequency keeping CPU at full utilisation
probable_causes.test: Profile CPU utilisation in firmware; identify task with highest runtime percentage

steps.instruction: Review deep-sleep entry code; ensure all GPIOs are configured to hold correct states and wake-up source is configured
steps.verification: Measured sleep current below 100 uA for ESP32 or per-chip specification

steps.instruction: Add transistor switch (or load switch IC) on peripheral power rail controlled by GPIO; disable before entering sleep
steps.verification: Peripheral current drops to zero during sleep; measured total sleep current meets target

steps.instruction: Replace polling loops with interrupt-driven or event-driven wake patterns to reduce active CPU time
steps.verification: CPU utilisation below 30% in idle state; average power consumption reduced by at least 40%

problems.name: Data Corruption in Flash
problems.description: Persistent data stored in flash memory becomes corrupted, causing configuration loss, invalid readings or boot failures.
visual_symptoms: Device boots with corrupted or default settings after power loss
Stored calibration data reads as 0xFF or random values
Filesystem check at boot reports errors or forces reformatting
CRC or checksum validation failures in application log

probable_causes.cause: Power loss during flash write operation leaving page in incomplete state
probable_causes.test: Simulate power cut during write in test environment; verify data integrity after restore

probable_causes.cause: Flash wear limit exceeded on frequently written sector causing unreliable writes
probable_causes.test: Read erase count from flash wear log if available; compare with flash manufacturer endurance specification

probable_causes.cause: Incorrect flash write sequence missing erase-before-write step
probable_causes.test: Review flash write code path to confirm erase is performed before every page write

steps.instruction: Implement CRC32 checksum on all stored data structures; validate checksum on read and fall back to defaults if invalid
steps.verification: Application detects and recovers gracefully from corruption without crashing

steps.instruction: Use a wear-levelling filesystem (LittleFS or SPIFFS with wear levelling) instead of raw flash writes
steps.verification: Filesystem erase counts distributed evenly across sectors; no single sector disproportionately worn

steps.instruction: Implement write-ahead logging or dual-bank storage with atomic commit to survive power loss during write
steps.verification: Power-cut simulation during write results in valid data recovery from backup bank on next boot
